title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'Young people and money: are we teaching the next generation the right financial habits? Write an article discussing whether young people today are good at managing money and what skills they need to develop.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Money Matters: Are Young People Financially Prepared?

In a world of contactless payments, buy-now-pay-later schemes, and Instagram advertising, managing money has never been more complicated — or more important. Yet financial education remains surprisingly absent from most school curricula. The result? A generation of young people entering adulthood without the basic skills to manage their finances effectively.

Studies consistently show that many young adults struggle with budgeting. The ease of online spending makes it tempting to buy impulsively, and credit cards can create a dangerous illusion of wealth. Many young people arrive at university without ever having set a budget, paid a bill, or understood how interest on debt works.

The solution is straightforward: financial literacy must be taught in schools. Basic budgeting, understanding debt, saving habits, and the principles of investment should be as fundamental as maths and English.

Parents also play a vital role. Encouraging teenagers to manage pocket money responsibly and discussing household finances openly can build sound habits that last a lifetime.

Money may not buy happiness, but knowing how to manage it certainly helps.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
Your student union has asked you to write a report on the financial difficulties facing students at your college. Include information about the main financial pressures students experience, how these affect their studies, and recommendations for support the college could provide.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Financial Difficulties Facing Students at Briarwood College

Introduction
This report examines the financial challenges experienced by students at Briarwood College, based on a survey of 160 respondents, and proposes measures to improve support.

Main Financial Pressures
The survey identified three primary financial concerns: the rising cost of accommodation, increasing food prices, and the expense of course materials and textbooks. Over 70% of respondents reported working part-time jobs to cover basic living costs, with some working more than 20 hours per week.

Impact on Studies
The consequences for academic performance are significant. Students who work long hours report higher levels of fatigue, reduced study time, and increased anxiety. Several respondents mentioned missing lectures due to work commitments, and some expressed considering dropping out of their courses entirely for financial reasons.

Recommendations
The college should expand its hardship fund and simplify the application process, which many students currently find overly complicated. Additionally, a textbook lending library, subsidised meal options in the canteen, and free financial counselling sessions would meaningfully reduce the burden on students in difficulty.

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice on a website:

'We want reviews of personal finance apps or budgeting tools. Have you used one that has helped you manage your money better? Write a review describing the app and saying whether you would recommend it.'

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
App Review: YNAB (You Need a Budget)

YNAB, which stands for You Need a Budget, is a personal finance app built around a simple but powerful idea: every pound you earn should have a specific job to do. Rather than simply tracking what you have already spent, YNAB encourages you to actively plan where your money will go before you spend it.

The app connects to your bank account and categorises your transactions automatically, though you can also enter them manually. The interface is clean and intuitive, and the built-in tutorials are genuinely helpful for beginners.

I have been using YNAB for eight months, and it has completely changed my relationship with money. In the first month alone, I identified several subscriptions I had forgotten about and cut unnecessary spending by nearly 20%.

The main drawback is the cost — at around £10 per month after a free trial — which feels ironic for a budgeting app. However, for most users, the savings quickly outweigh the subscription fee.

If you are serious about taking control of your finances, YNAB is outstanding. Rating: 4.5/5
